document: Final report on amending GLs on ICT risk and security (GDPR - Row 4)

sections.heading: On 27 November 2019, the EBA published the Guidelines on ICT and security risk management
sections.summary: EBA published ICT and security risk management guidelines on 27 November 2019, building on CRD and PSD2 provisions, replacing older security measures guidelines.
sections.source_quote: On 27 November 2019, the EBA published the Guidelines on ICT and security risk management (EBA/GL/2019/04) which were built on the provisions of Article 74 of Directive 2013/36/EU (CRD) and Article 95(3) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366 (PSD2).

sections.heading: DORA entered into force in January 2023 and will apply from 17 January 2025 onwards. DORA in-
sections.summary: EBA/GL/2025/02 Guidelines amend EBA/GL/2019/04 on ICT and security risk management, setting supervisory practices for competent authorities.
sections.source_quote: Guidelines set the EBA view of appropriate supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision or of how Union law should be applied in a particular area.

sections.heading: The entities within DORA's scope of action cover some of the PSPs within the scope of PSD2, namely
sections.summary: Competent authorities must notify EBA by 20.05.2025 whether they comply with EBA Guidelines or have reasons for non-compliance.
sections.source_quote: According to Article 16(3) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010, competent authorities must notify the EBA as to whether they comply or intend to comply with these guidelines, or otherwise with reasons for non-compliance, by 20.05.2025.

sections.heading: Article 7(4) of Directive (EU) 2022/2556, amended Article 95(1) of PSD2 by adding the following
sections.summary: Guidelines apply to competent authorities as defined in Article 4(2)(vii) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 and financial institutions as payment service providers under PSD2.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines are addressed to competent authorities as defined in Article 4 point (2)(vii) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 and to financial institutions as defined in Article 4(1) of Regulation No 1093/2010, which are payment service providers as referred to in Article 1(1) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366.

sections.heading: Article 2(2)(f) of DORA, explicitly excludes from the scope of action of DORA post office giro institu-
sections.summary: Guidelines specify security measures for payment service providers to manage operational and security risks under PSD2.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines specify requirements for the establishment, implementation and monitoring of the security measures that PSPs must take, in accordance with Article 95(1) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, to manage the operational and security risks relating to the payment services they provide.

sections.heading: In addition, National Competent Authorities have the possibility to subject PSPs that are not cov-
sections.summary: Guidelines address competent authorities and financial institutions as payment service providers, including those exempted under PSD2.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines are addressed to competent authorities as defined in Article 4 point (2)(vii) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 and to financial institutions as defined in Article 4(1) of Regulation No 1093/2010, which are payment service providers as defined in Article 1(1) point (a), point (b) and point (d) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, including natural or legal persons benefiting from an exemption pursuant to Article 32 or 33 of Directive (EU) 2015/2366 and legal persons exempted under Article 9 of Directive 2009/110/EC.

sections.heading: The separate proposal that the Commission published in June 2023 on the revision of PSD2 towards
sections.summary: Definitions in the guidelines are deleted, with new text specifying requirements for security measures.
sections.source_quote: The definitions as set out in paragraph 10 are deleted.

sections.heading: Credit institutions and investment firms as defined in point 3 of Article 4(1) of Regulation (EU) No
sections.summary: Credit institutions and investment firms covered by EBA Guidelines overlap with financial entities subject to DORA and related RTS on RMF.
sections.source_quote: Credit institutions and investment firms as defined in point 3 of Article 4(1) of Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, addressees of the EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management overlap with the financial entities subject to DORA and the related RTS on RMF.

sections.heading: The EBA carried out a gap analysis in May 2024 and arrived at the view that the majority of the gaps
sections.summary: EBA gap analysis in May 2024 found that most gaps in authentication methods and security policies were not material and covered by DORA.
sections.source_quote: The EBA carried out a gap analysis in May 2024 and arrived at the view that the majority of the gaps identified which relate to authentication methods, requirements for the information security policy and ICT operations management, were not material and holistically covered by the new DORA framework.

sections.heading: The definitions included in EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management overlap with DORA
sections.summary: Definitions in EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management overlap with DORA Article (3).
sections.source_quote: The definitions included in EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management overlap with DORA Article (3).

sections.heading: Guidelines
sections.summary: EBA/GL/2025/02 Guidelines amend EBA/GL/2019/04 on ICT and security risk management, setting supervisory practices for competent authorities.
sections.source_quote: Guidelines set the EBA view of appropriate supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision or of how Union law should be applied in a particular area.

sections.heading: Guidelines set the EBA view of appropriate supervisory practices within the European
sections.summary: EBA/GL/2025/02 Guidelines amend EBA/GL/2019/04 on ICT and security risk management, setting supervisory practices for competent authorities.
sections.source_quote: Guidelines set the EBA view of appropriate supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision or of how Union law should be applied in a particular area.

sections.heading: According to Article 16(3) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010, competent authorities
sections.summary: Competent authorities must notify EBA by 20.05.2025 whether they comply with EBA Guidelines or have reasons for non-compliance.
sections.source_quote: According to Article 16(3) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010, competent authorities must notify the EBA as to whether they comply or intend to comply with these guidelines, or otherwise with reasons for non-compliance, by 20.05.2025.

sections.heading: Notifications will be published on the EBA website, in line with Article 16(3).
sections.summary: Notifications of compliance with EBA Guidelines will be published on the EBA website by 20.05.2025.
sections.source_quote: Notifications will be published on the EBA website, in line with Article 16(3).

sections.heading: These guidelines are addressed to competent authorities as defined in Article 4 point
sections.summary: Guidelines apply to competent authorities as defined in Article 4(2)(vii) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 and financial institutions as payment service providers under PSD2.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines are addressed to competent authorities as defined in Article 4 point (2)(vii) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 and to financial institutions as defined in Article 4(1) of Regulation No 1093/2010, which are payment service providers as referred to in Article 1(1) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366.

sections.heading: The subject matter as set out in paragraphs 5 and 6 is replaced with the following:
sections.summary: Guidelines specify security measures for payment service providers to manage operational and security risks under PSD2.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines specify requirements for the establishment, implementation and monitoring of the security measures that PSPs must take, in accordance with Article 95(1) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, to manage the operational and security risks relating to the payment services they provide.

sections.heading: The scope of application as set out in paragraphs 7 and 8 is deleted.
sections.summary: The scope of application for the guidelines is deleted, with new text specifying requirements for security measures for payment service providers.
sections.source_quote: These Guidelines specify requirements for the establishment, implementation and monitoring of the security measures that PSPs must take, in accordance with Article 95(1) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, to manage the operational and security risks relating to the payment services they provide.

sections.heading: The addressees as set out in paragraph 9 are replaced by the following:
sections.summary: Guidelines address competent authorities and financial institutions as payment service providers, including those exempted under PSD2.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines are addressed to competent authorities as defined in Article 4 point (2)(vii) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 and to financial institutions as defined in Article 4(1) of Regulation No 1093/2010, which are payment service providers as defined in Article 1(1) point (a), point (b) and point (d) of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, including natural or legal persons benefiting from an exemption pursuant to Article 32 or 33 of Directive (EU) 2015/2366 and legal persons exempted under Article 9 of Directive 2009/110/EC.
